detailedsolution: Tendons must be very tough to handle the strong pull of contracting muscles.

questiontext: Which type of joint allows movement in only one plane or direction?
hint: Think of how a door opens.
detailedsolution: A hinge joint allows movement in one plane, similar to the hinge of a door.

questiontext: Which type of joint allows movement in all directions?
hint: Think of a joint that can move in a circle.
detailedsolution: Ball and socket joints allow movement in all directions: forward, backward, sideways, and circular.

questiontext: Which of the following is an example of a hinge joint?
hint: It's a joint in your leg that only bends one way.
detailedsolution: The knee and elbow are primary examples of hinge joints.

questiontext: Which of the following is an example of a ball and socket joint?
hint: This joint connects your leg to your pelvis.
detailedsolution: The hip and shoulder are examples of ball and socket joints.

questiontext: In a ball and socket joint, what fits into the 'cup-like' socket?
optionc: A rounded, ball-like end of a bone
hint: The name of the joint describes its shape.
detailedsolution: A ball and socket joint consists of a rounded end of one bone fitting into a socket of another.

questiontext: How does the movement of a hinge joint compare to a ball and socket joint?
optiona: Hinge joint moves in all directions
optionb: Ball and socket joint moves in one plane
optionc: Hinge joint moves in one plane only
hint: One is limited; the other is free.
detailedsolution: Hinge joints are limited to one plane, whereas ball and socket joints have a great range of motion.

questiontext: Which joint allows you to swing your arm in a complete circle?
hint: It is located where your arm meets your body.
detailedsolution: The shoulder is a ball and socket joint that allows for circular movement.

questiontext: The elbow is a hinge joint. Which activity does it mainly allow?
hint: Think of how you bring food to your mouth.
detailedsolution: The elbow hinge joint allows the arm to bend and straighten in one direction.

questiontext: Why is the shoulder joint said to be more 'mobile' than the knee joint?
optionb: It allows movement in all directions
hint: Mobility refers to the range of movement.
detailedsolution: As a ball and socket joint, the shoulder has a much wider range of motion than the hinge joint of the knee.

questiontext: A hinge joint is compared to the movement of which household object?
hint: The name is the same as the object.
detailedsolution: Hinge joints work exactly like the hinges on a door.

questiontext: Where is the shoulder joint located?
hint: It connects the upper arm bone to the shoulder blade.
detailedsolution: The shoulder joint is formed where the humerus fits into the scapula.

questiontext: Where is the hip joint located?
optionc: Between the femur and the pelvic girdle
hint: It's the joint at the top of your leg.
detailedsolution: The hip is a ball and socket joint connecting the thigh bone (femur) to the pelvis.

questiontext: In a hinge joint, what usually happens to one of the bones while the other moves?
hint: Think of a door frame vs. the door.
detailedsolution: In a hinge joint, one bone moves while the other stays in a fixed position.

questiontext: Which of these joints is a ball and socket joint?
hint: It allows your arm to move very freely.
detailedsolution: The shoulder and hip are the two main ball and socket joints in humans.

questiontext: Which joint allows you to kick a ball forward?
hint: It's a hinge joint in the leg.
detailedsolution: The knee allows for the back-and-forth motion needed to kick.

questiontext: How many planes of movement does a ball and socket joint allow?
hint: It is the most flexible type of joint.
detailedsolution: Ball and socket joints allow movement in multiple planes, including rotation.

questiontext: The 'ball' in the shoulder joint is the end of which bone?
hint: It's the bone of the upper arm.
detailedsolution: The rounded head of the humerus forms the 'ball' of the shoulder joint.

questiontext: The 'socket' in the hip joint is found in which bone?
hint: It's part of the hip bone.
detailedsolution: The pelvic girdle contains the socket for the femur's head.

questiontext: Which joint type is found at the knuckles?
hint: Your fingers bend like a door.
detailedsolution: Knuckles are often classified as hinge joints (or modified hinge joints) in primary science.

questiontext: What prevents the 'ball' from slipping out of the 'socket' easily?
hint: Think of the 'strings' holding joints together.
detailedsolution: Ligaments hold the bones together at the joint and provide stability.

questiontext: The knee is which type of joint?
hint: It works like a door.
detailedsolution: The knee is a typical hinge joint.

questiontext: Which joint type provides the greatest range of motion?
hint: It can move in all directions.
detailedsolution: Ball and socket joints provide the most extensive movement in the body.

questiontext: Why is the knee sometimes called a 'modified' hinge joint?
optionb: Because it has a knee cap (patella)
optionc: Because it is made of fat
optiond: Because it can move in circles
hint: Check the P.7 notes on 'Types of movable joints'.
detailedsolution: The knee is modified because the patella prevents it from turning around.

questiontext: The joint in the shoulder is?
hint: You can roll your shoulders.
detailedsolution: The shoulder is a ball and socket joint.

questiontext: What is the main limitation of a hinge joint?
optionb: It can only move in one direction
hint: Think of your elbow.
detailedsolution: A hinge joint is limited to movement in a single plane.

questiontext: Which joint is more likely to be used for locomotion (walking/running)?
hint: Think about your legs.
detailedsolution: Walking requires the hinge movement of the knee and the multi-directional movement of the hip.

questiontext: Which joint allows for 'circular movement'?
hint: This is movement 'all round'.
detailedsolution: Ball and socket joints allow for rotation and circular movement.

questiontext: The hip joint is formed by the femur and the?
hint: It's the large bone in your lower trunk.
detailedsolution: The hip is a ball and socket joint involving the pelvic girdle.

questiontext: What covers the 'ball' and 'socket' surfaces to reduce friction?
hint: It's a smooth, white cushion.
detailedsolution: Cartilage provides a smooth surface for the ball and socket to glide.

questiontext: The elbow allows your arm to?
hint: Think of doing a 'bicep curl'.
detailedsolution: The elbow is a hinge joint that allows for bending (flexing) and straightening (extending).

questiontext: How many ball and socket joints does a human have in total?
hint: Two shoulders and two hips.
detailedsolution: Humans have 4 major ball and socket joints (shoulders and hips).

questiontext: Which joint type is most suitable for throwing a ball?
hint: Throwing requires a wide circular motion.
detailedsolution: The multi-directional movement of the ball and socket joint (shoulder) is key for throwing.

questiontext: A hinge joint allows movement in?
hint: Like a book opening.
detailedsolution: Hinge joints are uni-axial, meaning they move in one plane.

questiontext: Where would you find a hinge joint in the upper limb?
hint: It's the middle joint of the arm.
detailedsolution: The elbow is the main hinge joint of the upper limb.

questiontext: What is the role of synovial fluid in a ball and socket joint?
optionb: To provide lubrication for all-round movement
hint: It acts as oil for the 'ball' and 'cup'.
detailedsolution: Synovial fluid is crucial for reducing friction during multi-directional movement.

questiontext: Can the hip move in more directions than the knee?
hint: Compare a hinge to a ball and socket.
detailedsolution: As a ball and socket joint, the hip has much more range than the knee hinge.

questiontext: Which joint is located between the humerus and the scapula?
hint: Upper arm to shoulder blade.
detailedsolution: The shoulder is the joint connecting the humerus to the scapula.

questiontext: What is the common feature of all hinge and ball and socket joints?
optionc: They allow movement in all directions
hint: They both have fluid.
detailedsolution: Both are types of synovial joints, meaning they allow movement and have fluid.

questiontext: If you can only move a joint 'back and forth', it is likely a?
hint: One direction only.
detailedsolution: Back and forth movement in one plane is the signature of a hinge joint.

questiontext: The shoulder joint is a meeting point for which two bones?
hint: Look at the shoulder anatomy.
detailedsolution: The shoulder joint specifically links the humerus and the scapula.

questiontext: What is the socket of the shoulder joint called (not in all notes, but in advanced P.7)?
hint: Starts with G.
detailedsolution: The head of the humerus fits into the glenoid cavity of the scapula.

questiontext: Which joint type is best for stability rather than range of motion?
hint: Less directions means more stability.
detailedsolution: Hinge joints are generally more stable but have less movement range than ball and socket.

questiontext: Movement in a 'circular form' is allowed by?
hint: Rotation in circles.
detailedsolution: Only ball and socket joints allow full circular rotation.

questiontext: The knee joint is found between the femur and the?
hint: The shin bone.
detailedsolution: The knee is the hinge joint connecting the thigh bone (femur) and shin bone (tibia).

questiontext: Which of these activities relies on ball and socket joints?
hint: Think of the arm rotation in water.
detailedsolution: Swimming requires the wide circular motion of the shoulder joint.

questiontext: Ball and socket joints are?
hint: They allow a lot of action.
detailedsolution: As synovial joints, they are highly movable.

questiontext: The hip joint is more stable but less mobile than the shoulder joint. Why?
hint: Think of holding weight vs. reaching out.
detailedsolution: The hip socket is deeper to support body weight, making it more stable.

questiontext: What allows the 'one plane' movement of the elbow?
hint: One bone has a hollow that matches the rounded end of the other.
detailedsolution: The interlocking shape of the humerus and ulna creates the hinge mechanism.

questiontext: Can a ball and socket joint move backward?
hint: Try moving your arm behind you.
detailedsolution: Yes, ball and socket joints allow movement in all directions, including backward.

questiontext: Which joint allows you to sit down by bending your legs?
hint: You need both the thigh and leg to bend.
detailedsolution: Sitting requires bending at the hip (ball and socket) and knee (hinge).

questiontext: Which bone forms the socket of the hip joint?
hint: It's the large bone in the lower trunk.
detailedsolution: The pelvic girdle contains a cup-like socket for the femur head.

questiontext: The knee joint is which type?
hint: Works like a door.
detailedsolution: The knee is a hinge joint.

questiontext: A joint that allows movement forward, backward, and sideways is a?
hint: Many directions.
detailedsolution: Ball and socket joints are characterized by multi-directional movement.

questiontext: What is the main advantage of the ball-and-socket joint's design?
optionb: It allows for a huge range of motion
hint: Think of why it is the most flexible.
detailedsolution: The fit of a rounded head into a cup allows for free movement in many planes.

questiontext: Which joint allows you to lift your leg sideways?
hint: It's a ball and socket joint.
detailedsolution: The hip joint allows for sideways (lateral) movement of the leg.

questiontext: Hinge joints are found at the knee and the?
hint: Another one-way bending joint.
detailedsolution: The elbow is the other major hinge joint in the body.

questiontext: What type of movement is 'flexion and extension'?
hint: Typical of hinge joints.
detailedsolution: Hinge joints primarily perform flexion (bending) and extension (straightening).

questiontext: Can the elbow move in a circle like the shoulder?
hint: Try it!
detailedsolution: The elbow is a hinge joint and cannot rotate in a full circle like the shoulder.

questiontext: Where is the 'ball' of the hip joint?
optiona: At the top of the femur
optionb: In the middle of the tibia
hint: It's the head of the thigh bone.
detailedsolution: The rounded top of the femur forms the ball of the hip joint.

questiontext: The shoulder joint provides room for?
optionb: Extensive muscular attachment and wide range of motion
hint: Think of the muscle-joint relationship.
detailedsolution: The design of the shoulder allows for many muscles to attach and move the arm freely.

questiontext: Which type of joint allows the rotation of one bone around another?
hint: Think of how your head turns.
detailedsolution: A pivot joint allows one bone to rotate or turn around another bone.

questiontext: Where is the primary example of a pivot joint found in the human body?
hint: It's what allows you to look left and right.
detailedsolution: In humans, the best example of a pivot joint is found in the neck.

questiontext: Which head movements are made possible by the pivot joint in the neck?
hint: Action for "Yes" and "No".
detailedsolution: Pivot joints in the neck allow us to nod our heads and shake them from side to side.

questiontext: In which type of joint do flat bones slide over each other?
hint: The name describes the sliding motion.
detailedsolution: Gliding joints consist of flat surfaces that slide or glide over one another.

questiontext: Where are gliding joints located in the human body?
hint: Think of the small bones in your hand and foot.
detailedsolution: Gliding joints are found between the carpals (wrist) and tarsals (ankle).

questiontext: Gliding joints are also known as?
hint: Think of a flat surface.
detailedsolution: Gliding joints are often called plane joints because of their flat surfaces.

questiontext: A pivot joint consists of a rounded end of one bone fitting into a ______ formed by another bone.
hint: Think of a key turning in a lock.
detailedsolution: A pivot joint is designed with one bone rotating inside a bony ring.

questiontext: Which bones are involved in the gliding joints of the wrist?
hint: These are the short bones of the hand area.
detailedsolution: The carpals in the wrist slide over each other to provide flexibility.

questiontext: Which bones are involved in the gliding joints of the ankle?
hint: These are the short bones of the foot area.
detailedsolution: Tarsals in the ankle form gliding joints that allow the foot to adjust positions.

questiontext: The pivot joint in the neck is specifically located between the first and second ______?
hint: Bones of the backbone.
detailedsolution: The pivot joint is between the first (Atlas) and second (Axis) cervical vertebrae.

questiontext: How much rotation does a gliding joint typically produce?
hint: The movement is mostly sliding.
detailedsolution: Gliding joints produce very little rotation; their primary motion is sliding.

questiontext: Which joint allows you to say "no" by turning your head?
hint: Rotation in the neck.
detailedsolution: Turning the head side-to-side is a rotational movement allowed by the pivot joint.

questiontext: What is the main physical feature of bones in a gliding joint?
hint: Flat surfaces slide best.
detailedsolution: Gliding joints are made of relatively flat bone surfaces.

questiontext: Which of these allows your wrist to move slightly in many directions?
hint: Small bones sliding together.
detailedsolution: The sliding of carpal bones allows the wrist to be flexible and adjust positions.

questiontext: The pivot joint is which type of joint?
hint: It allows the head to move.
detailedsolution: As a synovial joint, the pivot joint is classified as movable.

questiontext: Which vertebra is responsible for 'pivoting' the head?
hint: It acts as the axle or axis.
detailedsolution: The Axis (second cervical vertebra) allows for the rotation of the head.

questiontext: Why are gliding joints important when walking on uneven ground?
optiona: They lock the foot in place
optionb: They allow the ankle bones to slide and adjust
hint: Flexibility in the foot.
detailedsolution: Gliding joints in the tarsals allow the foot to adapt to different surfaces.

questiontext: Which joint allows you to move your head up and down as if saying "yes"?
hint: Movements of the neck.
detailedsolution: The pivot joint allows for both shaking and nodding of the head.

questiontext: If a joint allows bones to move 'past each other', it is a?
hint: Bones slide past.
detailedsolution: Sliding past each other is the defining movement of a gliding joint.

questiontext: Which of these is NOT a location for a gliding joint?
hint: Gliding happens between meeting bone surfaces.
detailedsolution: Gliding joints are between small bones or vertebrae; the humerus is a single bone.

questiontext: Does a pivot joint allow you to swing your arm in a circle?
hint: Rotation is different from circular swinging.
detailedsolution: Only ball and socket joints allow full circular swings.

questiontext: The carpals are found in the ______?
hint: Part of the upper limb.
detailedsolution: The carpals are the 8 bones that form the wrist.

questiontext: The tarsals are found in the ______?
hint: Part of the lower limb.
detailedsolution: The tarsals are the bones that form the ankle.

questiontext: Which joint is most limited in its range of motion among these?
hint: It only allows small sliding movements.
detailedsolution: Gliding joints have the most restricted movement compared to other movable joints.

questiontext: Rotation of certain body parts is the main work of?
hint: Think of a spinning movement.
detailedsolution: Pivot joints are specifically designed for rotation.

questiontext: Which type of joint helps in fine adjustments of the hand?
hint: Small bones sliding.
detailedsolution: The gliding joints of the carpals allow for the fine flexibility of the wrist.

questiontext: What is the role of synovial fluid in a gliding joint?
hint: It acts as oil for sliding.
detailedsolution: Even small sliding movements need lubrication to prevent wear and tear.

questiontext: Can the gliding joint in the ankle move the foot in a full circle?
hint: The movement is limited.
detailedsolution: Ankle movements are complex but gliding joints only allow sliding, not full rotation.

questiontext: The first neck vertebra that the head rests on is the?
hint: Named after a giant who held up the world.
detailedsolution: The Atlas (first vertebra) supports the skull and meets the Axis to form the pivot.

questiontext: Which movement is shared by both the wrist and the ankle?
hint: Both have small flat bones.
detailedsolution: Both areas contain gliding joints (between carpals and tarsals).

questiontext: A pivot joint allows ______ movement?
hint: Think of turning a knob.
detailedsolution: Pivot joints are the "rotation" specialists of the body.

questiontext: Which joint allows you to look over your shoulder?
hint: You have to rotate your neck.
detailedsolution: The pivot joint in the neck allows you to rotate your head to look sideways.

questiontext: Gliding movements produce ______ rotation?
hint: Check the notes on 'Gliding joint'.
detailedsolution: Gliding movements produce very little rotation; they are mostly linear slides.

questiontext: Are the joints between the carpals movable?
hint: You can move your wrist.
detailedsolution: Yes, they are movable (synovial) gliding joints.

questiontext: Where do you find the Axis and Atlas vertebrae?
hint: They are cervical vertebrae.
detailedsolution: These are the first two bones of the spine, located in the neck.

questiontext: What prevents the vertebrae in the neck from rubbing together?
hint: Typical of all movable joints.
detailedsolution: Like other synovial joints, the neck pivot joint has fluid and cartilage.

questiontext: Which joint is used more when typing on a keyboard?
hint: Think of your hand position.
detailedsolution: The wrist joints allow for the flexible positioning needed to type.

questiontext: How many carpals form the gliding joints in one wrist?
hint: Total small bones in the wrist.
detailedsolution: There are 8 carpal bones in the wrist that slide against each other.

questiontext: How many tarsals are found in one ankle according to your notes?
hint: Small bones of the foot.
detailedsolution: The notes identify 8 tarsal bones in the ankle.

questiontext: What type of joint allows the small bones of the hand to shift when you make a fist?
hint: Small bones sliding.
detailedsolution: The gliding joints between carpals allow the wrist and hand to change shape.

questiontext: The neck joint is which type?
hint: It moves in circles and side-to-side.
detailedsolution: The neck contains a movable pivot joint.

questiontext: Which joint allows you to rotate your wrist?
hint: Bones slide past each other.
detailedsolution: The sliding of the carpal bones allows for the rotation and bending of the wrist.

questiontext: In a pivot joint, the rounded bone rotates around its own ______?
hint: A central point of turning.
detailedsolution: Pivot joints allow a bone to spin around its own longitudinal axis.

questiontext: Turning a screwdriver relies most on which joint type in the arm/hand?
hint: The wrist must rotate/slide.
detailedsolution: Rotating the wrist to turn objects involves the gliding of carpal bones.

questiontext: Where are 'plane joints' typically found?
hint: Another name for gliding joints.
detailedsolution: Wrist and ankle joints are often referred to as plane joints.

questiontext: What type of tissue is found between the gliding bones?
hint: To stop them from rubbing.
detailedsolution: The surfaces of the gliding carpals and tarsals are covered in cartilage.

questiontext: The pivot joint is found in the ______?
hint: It's where you turn your head.
detailedsolution: Primary location for the pivot joint is the neck.

questiontext: What allows the head to move back and forth?
optionb: The pivot joint of the first/second vertebrae
hint: Check the 'Pivot joint' notes.
detailedsolution: The joint between the Atlas and Axis allows the head to move back and forth and rotate.

questiontext: Which joint type is best for maintaining balance on a moving boat?
hint: Small adjustments in the feet.
detailedsolution: The gliding tarsals allow the feet to shift and maintain balance.

questiontext: Is the joint in the ankle a hinge joint?
hint: It is a gliding joint.
detailedsolution: In the P.7 notes, the ankle is identified as a gliding joint.

questiontext: Rotation is the characteristic movement of which joint?
hint: Think of a spinning axle.
detailedsolution: Pivot joints are the only ones whose primary job is rotation.

questiontext: The movement of the wrist is made of ______ sliding over each other?
hint: Check the definition of gliding joints.
detailedsolution: Gliding joints are where flat bones slide past each other.

questiontext: Which joint helps us nod our heads?
hint: Action of the neck.
detailedsolution: The pivot joint in the neck facilitates nodding.

questiontext: Wrist and ankle joints are examples of?
hint: They allow movement.
detailedsolution: Both are types of movable (synovial) joints.

questiontext: What structure allows the rotational movement of the neck?
optionb: A ring formed by the other bone
hint: Check the Pivot joint definition.
detailedsolution: One bone spins inside a bony ring formed by the other.

questiontext: Gliding joints are found in the wrist and ______?
hint: The other "hinge-like" flexible area.
detailedsolution: Gliding joints are located in the wrist (hand) and ankle (foot).

questiontext: Why are gliding joints useful for the hand?
optionb: They allow for flexible wrist motion
hint: Allows for complex hand positions.
detailedsolution: The sliding carpals allow for the wrist's flexibility.

questiontext: A pivot joint is a type of ______ joint?
hint: Because it has a capsule and fluid.
detailedsolution: All movable joints (including pivot and gliding) are synovial joints.

questiontext: Which joint would you use to shake your head to say "no"?
hint: Neck rotation.
detailedsolution: Shaking the head is a rotation made possible by the pivot joint.

questiontext: Bones of the tarsals and carpals are?
hint: Check the 'Gliding joints' section.
detailedsolution: Gliding joints involve flat bones that slide over each other.

questiontext: What are muscles?
optionb: Elastic fibres or bundles of flesh
hint: They can stretch and contract.
detailedsolution: Muscles are elastic substances or bundles of flesh found in the body that produce movement.

questiontext: Which tissue connects muscles to bones?
hint: Think of 'MTB' (Muscle-Tendon-Bone).
detailedsolution: Muscles are connected to bones by tough fibrous tissues called tendons.

questiontext: How many main types of muscles are there in the human body?
hint: One is voluntary, one is involuntary, and one is special for the heart.
detailedsolution: There are three types: Voluntary (Skeletal), Involuntary (Smooth), and Cardiac muscles.

questiontext: What is the main action performed by muscles?
hint: Think of how your bicep moves.
detailedsolution: Muscles produce movement by contracting (shortening) and relaxing.

questiontext: Which muscles are under our conscious control?
hint: You can 'will' them to move.
detailedsolution: Voluntary muscles are those whose movement can be controlled by our will.

questiontext: What is another name for voluntary muscles?
hint: They are attached to the skeleton.
detailedsolution: Voluntary muscles are also called skeletal muscles because they are attached to bones.

questiontext: Which of these is an example of a voluntary muscle?
hint: You use it to lift objects.
detailedsolution: The biceps in the arm are voluntary muscles that we control.

questiontext: Which characteristic describes voluntary muscles?
optionc: They are not attached to bones
hint: Think of how your legs feel after a long race.
detailedsolution: Voluntary muscles get tired quickly after heavy work or exercise.

questiontext: Which muscles work automatically without our control?
hint: You don't have to think about them.
detailedsolution: Involuntary muscles move automatically and are not controlled by will.

questiontext: Involuntary muscles are also known as?
hint: They have a smooth appearance.
detailedsolution: Involuntary muscles are commonly called smooth muscles.

questiontext: Where would you find involuntary (smooth) muscles?
optionb: In the walls of the stomach
hint: Look for internal organs.
detailedsolution: Involuntary muscles are found in the walls of internal organs like the stomach and intestines.

questiontext: Which statement is true about involuntary muscles?
optionb: They work continuously without getting tired easily
optionc: They are attached to the skull
optiond: We can stop them whenever we want
hint: Think of your stomach digesting food.
detailedsolution: Involuntary muscles work continuously and do not tire as quickly as skeletal muscles.

questiontext: Where is the cardiac muscle found?
hint: 'Cardiac' refers to the heart.
detailedsolution: Cardiac muscle is unique and found only in the walls of the heart.

questiontext: What makes cardiac muscle special?
optiona: It only works when we are awake
optionb: It contracts rhythmically throughout life without getting tired
optionc: It is attached to the ribs
hint: It must pump blood every second.
detailedsolution: Cardiac muscles have the capacity to contract and relax rhythmically for a lifetime without tiring.

questiontext: When does the cardiac muscle stop working?
optionc: Only when the person is dead
hint: It never takes a break.
detailedsolution: The heart muscle only stops when life ends.

questiontext: Which of these is an involuntary muscle action?
hint: It happens without you thinking about it.
detailedsolution: The muscles of the eyelid help in blinking, which is an automatic/involuntary action.

questiontext: Which type of muscle combines structures of both voluntary and involuntary muscles?
hint: It is a very unique muscle type.
detailedsolution: Cardiac muscle combines characteristics of both types to be strong yet automatic.

questiontext: The muscles used for walking are?
hint: You choose to walk.
detailedsolution: Walking involves skeletal (voluntary) muscles like those in the legs.

questiontext: Muscles in the walls of blood vessels are?
hint: They control blood flow automatically.
detailedsolution: Blood vessels contain smooth (involuntary) muscles.

questiontext: Why are skeletal muscles called 'striped' muscles in some books?
optionb: Because of their appearance under a microscope
optionc: Because they are found in zebras
hint: It refers to their physical pattern.
detailedsolution: Skeletal muscles have a striped or striated appearance under a microscope.

questiontext: The triceps are located in the ______?
hint: They are at the back of the upper arm.
detailedsolution: The triceps are voluntary muscles found in the arm.

questiontext: What happens when a muscle contracts?
hint: Think of your bicep bulging.
detailedsolution: Contraction is the shortening of muscle fibres to pull on bones.

questiontext: Which system of the body is moved by skeletal muscles?
hint: Bones and muscles work together.
detailedsolution: Skeletal muscles are attached to bones to move the skeletal system.

questiontext: The muscle in the uterus that helps in childbirth is?
hint: It works automatically during labor.
detailedsolution: The uterus is made of smooth (involuntary) muscle.

questiontext: Which muscle is found in the calf of the leg?
hint: It's at the back of your lower leg.
detailedsolution: The calf muscle is a skeletal muscle used for walking and standing.

questiontext: Involuntary muscles move food through the digestive tract by a process called?
hint: It's a wave-like motion.
detailedsolution: Peristalsis is the automatic movement of food caused by involuntary muscles.

questiontext: Skeletal muscles form the ______ of the body?
hint: They make up most of our body mass.
detailedsolution: The majority of the body's flesh is made up of skeletal muscles.

questiontext: The heart is made of ______ muscle?
hint: Special name for heart tissue.
detailedsolution: The heart is composed entirely of cardiac muscle.

questiontext: Which muscle helps in the storage of oxygen in the body?
hint: Check the functions of muscles list.
detailedsolution: Muscles (specifically skeletal ones) help in the storage of oxygen.

questiontext: Muscles of the urinary bladder are?
hint: You can't control the wall of the bladder directly.
detailedsolution: The bladder is lined with smooth (involuntary) muscle.

questiontext: Which muscle type is attached to the skeleton?
hint: The name gives it away.
detailedsolution: Skeletal muscles are those attached to the bones.

questiontext: How does the brain control voluntary muscles?
hint: Signals travel through the nervous system.
detailedsolution: The brain sends signals through nerves to tell voluntary muscles to move.

questiontext: Which muscle types are found in the alimentary canal?
hint: They push food down.
detailedsolution: The walls of the alimentary canal (gut) are made of smooth muscle.

questiontext: Which muscle type works while you are sleeping?
hint: Your heart and breathing continue.
detailedsolution: Both involuntary and cardiac muscles work automatically even when we sleep.

questiontext: Which muscles produce heat to maintain body temperature?
hint: Movement produces warmth.
detailedsolution: The contraction of skeletal muscles produces heat as a byproduct.

questiontext: Muscles are bundles of ______ substances?
hint: They can stretch.
detailedsolution: Muscles are described as bundles of elastic substances.

questiontext: If you want to raise your hand, which muscle type do you use?
hint: It's a conscious choice.
detailedsolution: Raising your hand is a voluntary action.

questiontext: The rhythmic contraction of the heart is called?
hint: You can feel it in your chest.
detailedsolution: A heartbeat is the rhythmic contraction and relaxation of the cardiac muscle.

questiontext: Which muscles are NOT attached to bones?
hint: They are in the walls of organs.
detailedsolution: Involuntary muscles are generally located on body organs, not attached to bones.

questiontext: Which muscle is found in the upper arm?
hint: It's at the front of the arm.
detailedsolution: The biceps is a well-known voluntary muscle of the arm.

questiontext: What is the main function of muscles in the blood vessels?
hint: Check the functions of smooth muscles.
detailedsolution: Smooth muscles in arteries help regulate and control blood pressure.

questiontext: The diaphragm used in breathing is an example of?
hint: It works automatically.
detailedsolution: The diaphragm is an involuntary muscle that helps in breathing.

questiontext: Which muscle type has a 'striped' look?
hint: Another name for it.
detailedsolution: Skeletal muscles are often called striped or striated muscles.

questiontext: Where do the movements of the cardiac muscle originate?
hint: Check the Cardiac muscle section.
detailedsolution: Cardiac muscle movements originate from the muscle itself.

questiontext: Which muscles are located on internal body organs?
hint: Organs like the stomach.
detailedsolution: Involuntary muscles are found on the surface of body organs.

questiontext: When you eat, which muscles push food to the stomach?
hint: It happens automatically.
detailedsolution: Involuntary muscles in the esophagus push food down.

questiontext: Why are muscles important for posture?
optionb: They maintain body positions by supporting joints
hint: Think of sitting upright.
detailedsolution: Muscles maintain proper body postures by supporting the skeletal structure.

questiontext: Which muscle helps in the exchange of gases in the body?
hint: Think about breathing.
detailedsolution: Muscles involved in breathing facilitate the exchange of gases in the lungs.

questiontext: Can we control our heartbeat?
hint: It's automatic.
detailedsolution: The heart is made of cardiac muscle, which is involuntary.

questiontext: Which muscle type is responsible for pumping blood?
hint: It's the heart's main job.
detailedsolution: The cardiac muscle provides the force needed to pump blood throughout the body.

questiontext: Muscles of the eye lids are?
hint: They help in automatic blinking.
detailedsolution: Blinking is an automatic action performed by involuntary muscles.

questiontext: Skeletal muscles are connected to bones by?
hint: Muscle to Bone.
detailedsolution: Tendons are the tissues that anchor muscles to the skeleton.

questiontext: Which muscle type allows us to perform heavy work?
hint: Think of lifting loads.
detailedsolution: Skeletal muscles enable humans to perform physical labor.

questiontext: What is the effect of cardiac muscle relaxing?
hint: Think of the heart as a pump.
detailedsolution: When the cardiac muscle relaxes, the heart chambers fill with blood.

questiontext: How do enzymes relate to muscles?
optiona: Muscles make enzymes to break down glucose for energy
hint: Check the "Exercises are important" section.
detailedsolution: Muscle tissues produce enzymes to break down glucose and fatty acids for energy.

questiontext: Which muscle type is found in the intestines?
hint: It works on your food.
detailedsolution: The intestines are made of smooth muscle for automatic digestion.

questiontext: Muscles join bones together and give extra support to?
hint: They help ligaments keep joints stable.
detailedsolution: Muscles provide extra support to the joints they surround.

questiontext: Which characteristic describes involuntary muscles?
optionb: They are controlled by the brain's will
optionc: They only work when we exercise
optiond: They are attached to the femur
hint: They move on their own.
detailedsolution: Involuntary muscles are defined by their automatic, non-controlled movement.

questiontext: Muscles only ______ and ______?
hint: The two states of muscle action.
detailedsolution: Muscle function is limited to contraction and relaxation.

questiontext: The brain has NO control over which muscles?
hint: They work independently of our thoughts.
detailedsolution: The brain does not consciously control involuntary or cardiac muscles.

questiontext: What are antagonistic muscles?
optionb: Muscles that work in pairs and have opposite effects
optiond: Muscles found only in the head
hint: Think of a pair working against each other.
detailedsolution: Antagonistic muscles are muscles that work in pairs and have an opposite effect on each other.

questiontext: In an antagonistic pair, when one muscle contracts, what does the other do?
hint: They work in opposite ways.
detailedsolution: When one muscle in an antagonistic pair is shortened (contracted), the other must lengthen (relax).

questiontext: Which pair of muscles works together to move the arm?
hint: They are in your upper arm.
detailedsolution: The biceps and triceps are the primary antagonistic pair in the upper arm.

questiontext: What happens to the biceps when you bend your arm at the elbow?
hint: They bulge and get shorter.
detailedsolution: To bend the arm, the biceps muscle contracts to pull the forearm up.

questiontext: When the arm is bent at the elbow, what is the state of the triceps?
hint: They are the partner of the bicep.
detailedsolution: While the biceps contract to bend the arm, the triceps must relax.

questiontext: What happens to the triceps when you straighten your arm?
hint: They pull the arm back into a straight line.
detailedsolution: To straighten the arm, the triceps muscle contracts while the biceps relax.

questiontext: When the arm is straight, what is the state of the biceps?
hint: Opposite of the triceps action.
detailedsolution: When the arm is straightened, the biceps are in a relaxed state.

questiontext: Why are biceps called 'flexor' muscles?
optiond: Because they are in the leg
hint: Think of "flexing" your muscle.
detailedsolution: Biceps are flexors because their contraction causes the arm to bend at the joint.

questiontext: Why are triceps called 'extensor' muscles?
hint: To extend means to straighten.
detailedsolution: Triceps are extensors because their contraction straightens the arm.

questiontext: Which muscle pair is used to bend and straighten the knee?
hint: These are the big muscles in your thigh.
detailedsolution: The quadriceps and hamstrings work as an antagonistic pair for leg movement.

questiontext: To straighten the knee, which muscle must contract?
hint: This is the muscle on the front of the thigh.
detailedsolution: The quadriceps contract to straighten the leg at the knee.

questiontext: To bend the knee, which muscle must contract?
hint: This muscle is on the back of the thigh.
detailedsolution: The hamstrings contract to pull the lower leg upward, bending the knee.

questiontext: Muscles can only pull, they cannot ______?
hint: Think about how a rope works.
detailedsolution: Muscles produce movement only by pulling on bones; they cannot push them.

questiontext: Why must muscles work in pairs?
optionb: Because they can only pull in one direction
hint: Think about how a bone is moved back to its place.
detailedsolution: Since a muscle only pulls, a second muscle is needed to pull the bone back in the opposite direction.

questiontext: Where are the biceps located?
hint: The 'bulge' you show when strong.
detailedsolution: Biceps are located at the front of the upper arm.

questiontext: Where are the triceps located?
hint: Behind the biceps.
detailedsolution: Triceps are located at the back of the upper arm.

questiontext: Which muscle is the antagonistic partner to the hamstrings?
hint: Front of thigh vs back of thigh.
detailedsolution: The quadriceps and hamstrings form the antagonistic pair of the thigh.

questiontext: What happens to the length of a muscle when it contracts?
hint: Think of a rubber band being pulled.
detailedsolution: Contraction results in the shortening and thickening of the muscle fiber.

questiontext: If the quadriceps relax and the hamstrings contract, the leg will?
hint: Action of the back thigh muscle.
detailedsolution: Contraction of the hamstrings causes the leg to bend.

questiontext: If the hamstrings relax and the quadriceps contract, the leg will?
hint: Action of the front thigh muscle.
detailedsolution: Contraction of the quadriceps causes the leg to straighten/extend.

questiontext: The biceps pull on which part of the body to bend the arm?
hint: It pulls the lower arm up.
detailedsolution: Biceps are attached to the forearm bones to pull them toward the shoulder.

questiontext: What is the effect of the triceps contracting on the elbow joint?
hint: Extend means to move out.
detailedsolution: The triceps act as extensors to straighten the elbow joint.

questiontext: Biceps and Triceps are what kind of muscles?
hint: You control them to move your arm.
detailedsolution: They are skeletal muscles that we move by our own will.

questiontext: During a daily routine, why do we often use our biceps more than our triceps?
optionb: Because of lifting objects against gravity
hint: Check the note in the Tekule PDF (Page 19).
detailedsolution: Lifting things toward our body against gravity uses the biceps more frequently.

questiontext: Which muscle is an extensor of the arm?
hint: Straightens the arm.
detailedsolution: The triceps muscle is the extensor of the elbow joint.

questiontext: Which muscle is a flexor of the arm?
hint: Bends the arm.
detailedsolution: The biceps muscle is the flexor of the elbow joint.

questiontext: If a person cannot straighten their arm, which muscle might be injured?
hint: Which one is the extensor?
detailedsolution: An injury to the triceps would make it difficult to straighten the arm.

questiontext: If a person cannot bend their knee, which antagonistic muscle is likely failing to contract?
hint: The one that pulls the leg up.
detailedsolution: The hamstrings are responsible for bending the leg at the knee joint.

questiontext: What state is a muscle in when it is elongated and soft?
hint: Opposite of hard/short.
detailedsolution: A relaxed muscle is soft and in its elongated state.

questiontext: The word 'antagonistic' in science implies muscles that?
optiona: Help each other do the same thing
hint: Like characters in a story who are enemies.
detailedsolution: Antagonistic muscles work against each other to produce controlled movement.

questiontext: Which muscle is located at the front of the thigh?
hint: It's the large leg muscle group.
detailedsolution: The quadriceps are found on the anterior (front) part of the thigh.

questiontext: Which muscle is located at the back of the thigh?
hint: Partner to the quad.
detailedsolution: The hamstrings are found on the posterior (back) part of the thigh.

questiontext: What is the scientific reason muscles are attached in pairs to joints?
optionb: Because one pulls the bone and the other pulls it back
hint: A single muscle can only move a bone in one direction.
detailedsolution: Pairs allow for two-way movement at a joint by pulling in opposite directions.

questiontext: When you lift a heavy bucket, which muscle in your arm contracts?
hint: Bending the arm to lift.
detailedsolution: Lifting toward the shoulder requires the biceps flexor to contract.

questiontext: In the leg, which muscle acts as the extensor of the knee?
hint: Straightens the leg.
detailedsolution: The quadriceps extend the knee joint.

questiontext: In the leg, which muscle acts as the flexor of the knee?
hint: Bends the leg.
detailedsolution: The hamstrings flex the knee joint.

questiontext: Does the bicep work alone to move the arm?
hint: It always needs its partner.
detailedsolution: No, the biceps works with the triceps to move the arm.

questiontext: When jumping, which thigh muscle contracts to push you off the ground by straightening the legs?
hint: Straightening the leg action.
detailedsolution: The quadriceps contract to straighten the leg during a jump.

questiontext: A muscle becomes ______ and ______ when it relaxes?
hint: The relaxed state.
detailedsolution: A relaxed muscle is elongated and soft to the touch.

questiontext: The elbow is a hinge joint moved by which muscle pair?
hint: Think of the upper arm.
detailedsolution: The biceps and triceps work over the elbow joint.

questiontext: Which muscle is found at the back of the upper arm?
hint: Behind the bone.
detailedsolution: The triceps are the muscles at the back of the arm.

questiontext: If you are pushing a heavy door open with a straight arm, which muscle is likely contracting?
hint: Straight arm action.
detailedsolution: Keeping the arm straight and pushing involves triceps contraction.

questiontext: What connects the biceps muscle to the scapula and the forearm?
hint: Muscle to bone connection.
detailedsolution: Tendons at each end of the bicep attach it to the shoulder and forearm.

questiontext: Can muscles push bones?
hint: They only have one type of force.
detailedsolution: Muscles can only pull; they never push.

questiontext: The action of bending the leg while walking is caused by?
hint: Bending the knee.
detailedsolution: The hamstrings contract to bend the leg while walking.

questiontext: When a muscle contracts, it exerts a force that ______ on the bone?
hint: The fundamental action of a muscle.
detailedsolution: Muscles pull on bones to move them.

questiontext: Which muscle is the 'partner' of the biceps?
hint: The other upper arm muscle.
detailedsolution: The triceps is the antagonistic partner of the biceps.

questiontext: The triceps contract when the hand is?
hint: Action of the extensor.
detailedsolution: Triceps contract to straighten the arm.

questiontext: Why are flexors and extensors necessary for every movable joint?
optionb: To allow movement in opposite directions
hint: Think of going there and coming back.
detailedsolution: Joints need opposing muscle pairs to move a limb in two directions.

questiontext: The quadriceps and hamstrings are muscles of the?
hint: The big thigh muscles.
detailedsolution: They are located in the thigh to move the leg.

questiontext: To pull your heel toward your buttock, which muscle contracts?
hint: Bending the knee fully.
detailedsolution: The hamstrings pull the lower leg back and up.

questiontext: What is the state of the biceps when the triceps are contracted?
hint: They work in opposition.
detailedsolution: The biceps must relax for the triceps to straighten the arm.

questiontext: Which muscle is located at the front of the upper arm?
hint: Visible when you "show your muscles".
detailedsolution: The biceps is at the front of the upper arm.

questiontext: Which antagonistic pair is responsible for kicking a ball?
hint: Thigh muscles.
detailedsolution: The quads and hams control the leg's swing and strike.

questiontext: What would happen if both muscles in an antagonistic pair contracted at the same time?
optiona: The limb would move very fast
optionb: The limb would stay still and be stiff
hint: They would pull against each other with equal force.
detailedsolution: Opposing contractions would result in a locked, immobile joint.

questiontext: Flexor muscles ______ the joint?
hint: Check the definition of flexor.
detailedsolution: Flexors contract to bend a joint.

questiontext: Extensor muscles ______ the joint?
hint: Check the definition of extensor.
detailedsolution: Extensors contract to straighten a joint.

questiontext: When the biceps contract, they pull on which bone(s)?
hint: Bones of the forearm.
detailedsolution: Biceps pull the forearm toward the shoulder.

questiontext: Which muscle pulls the leg straight when you stand up from a chair?
hint: Straightening the leg at the knee.
detailedsolution: Standing up involves the extension of the knee by the quadriceps.

questiontext: What is the opposite of 'Flexion'?
hint: Bending vs. Straightening.
detailedsolution: Extension is the opposite of flexion (bending).

questiontext: What is posture?
optionb: The way a person positions their body
hint: It is about how you sit, stand, or walk.
detailedsolution: Posture is the position in which you hold your body while standing, sitting, or lying down.

questiontext: In which activities should we maintain correct posture?
hint: Posture applies to almost every physical activity.
detailedsolution: Good posture is required for sitting, standing, walking, and running.

questiontext: Which of these is a rule for good sitting posture?
hint: Think of a straight back.
detailedsolution: To maintain good posture, you should always sit upright without bending your spine.

questiontext: Where should your feet be when sitting correctly on a chair?
optionc: Both feet flat on the floor
hint: Stability comes from the ground.
detailedsolution: Proper sitting posture requires placing both feet flat on the floor.

questiontext: When sitting, you should put all your body weight on?
hint: Even weight distribution is key.
detailedsolution: Good sitting posture involves distributing weight equally on both buttocks.

questiontext: What should you NOT do with your ankles and knees while sitting?
hint: Tightness causes muscle strain.
detailedsolution: One should not tighten the ankles and knees during sitting or movement.

questiontext: Which of these is a benefit of good posture?
optionb: It helps the skeleton develop properly
hint: Think of how a straight plant grows better.
detailedsolution: Good posture is important because it helps the skeleton and bones grow in the correct shape.

questiontext: How does good posture affect your appearance?
optionb: It makes you look smart and confident
hint: A straight back looks better.
detailedsolution: Maintaining good posture makes an individual look smart and healthy.

questiontext: Which body organs function better when you have good posture?
hint: Internal organs need space.
detailedsolution: Good posture allows internal body organs to function properly by providing enough space.

questiontext: What is a danger of sitting while bending forward for a long time?
hint: Bones take the shape of the position you stay in.
detailedsolution: If we do not sit properly, our bones can grow in abnormal or bent shapes.

questiontext: Bad posture can lead to pain in which two areas?
hint: Common complaints of people who slouch.
detailedsolution: Improper positioning of the body causes chest pain and backache.

questiontext: Why does bad posture lead to indigestion?
optionb: It squeezes the stomach and intestines
hint: Think of the digestive tract being crushed.
detailedsolution: Bad posture compresses the abdomen, which can lead to indigestion.

questiontext: Which of these activities leads to bad posture?
optionb: Sleeping while bending some body parts
hint: Think of sleeping in a curved position.
detailedsolution: Sleeping in awkward, bent positions can lead to postural deformities.

questiontext: Waling and running while bending forward causes?
hint: Moving while hunched is harmful.
detailedsolution: Bending forward while walking or running is a habit that leads to bad posture.

questiontext: Correct posture helps to prevent skeletal ______?
hint: A deformity is an abnormal shape.
detailedsolution: Maintaining good posture prevents bones from growing into abnormal shapes.

questiontext: How often should you brush your teeth to keep them healthy?
hint: Morning and before bed.
detailedsolution: The notes suggest brushing and flossing teeth twice a day.

questiontext: Besides brushing, what else helps clean between teeth?
hint: It is a thin string used for cleaning.
detailedsolution: Flossing is a health habit that helps keep teeth and gums healthy.

questiontext: Which mineral is important for both strong bones and strong teeth?
hint: Found in milk and green vegetables.
detailedsolution: Calcium is the main mineral required for the health of teeth and bones.

questiontext: Why should we go for dental checkups regularly?
optionb: To prevent and treat tooth decay early
hint: Prevention is better than cure.
detailedsolution: Regular visits to the dentist help in maintaining dental hygiene and health.

questiontext: Which part of the digestive process is improved by good sitting posture during meals?
hint: The stomach needs to be in a natural position.
detailedsolution: Correct posture while eating allows the stomach to process food effectively.

questiontext: Which of these makes a person look smart?
hint: Standing tall.
detailedsolution: One of the advantages of good body posture is that it makes one look smart.

questiontext: Good posture strengthens the muscles of the stomach and the ______?
hint: Think of the muscle used for breathing.
detailedsolution: Correct posture strengthens vital muscles like the diaphragm.

questiontext: Bad posture leads to poor blood ______ in the body?
hint: Think of blood flowing like water in a squeezed hose.
detailedsolution: Slouching can restrict blood flow, leading to poor circulation.

questiontext: Teeth are part of the skeletal system because they are ______?
hint: They are similar to bones in composition.
detailedsolution: Teeth, like bones, are hard structures that rely on calcium for strength.

questiontext: What happens to the lungs if you sit hunched over?
optionb: They are compressed and cannot expand fully
hint: They need space to fill with air.
detailedsolution: Bad posture restricts the chest cavity, making it harder for lungs to expand.

questiontext: A child who always stands while bending forward might develop a ______ spine?
hint: Bones are still growing in children.
detailedsolution: In children, bones are soft and can grow into abnormal shapes due to bad posture.

questiontext: Which habit keeps both bones and teeth strong?
optionb: Eating a balanced diet with calcium
hint: Nutrition is the foundation.
detailedsolution: A balanced diet rich in minerals like calcium is vital for bones and teeth.

questiontext: Why is it wrong to 'tighten' your knees while standing for a long time?
optionb: It causes muscle fatigue and poor circulation
hint: Locked joints are stressful for the body.
detailedsolution: Tightening joints during posture leads to unnecessary muscle strain.

questiontext: Posture is specifically the position of the ______ in everything we do?
hint: Body as a whole.
detailedsolution: Posture refers to the alignment and positioning of the entire body.

questiontext: What is the effect of good posture on back pain?
optiond: It makes pain move to the head
hint: Straight spines are happy spines.
detailedsolution: Correct alignment of the spine prevents the strain that causes backaches.

questiontext: Flossing should be done to remove ______ between teeth?
hint: Cleaning the gaps.
detailedsolution: Flossing removes debris that a toothbrush cannot reach.

questiontext: How does bad posture contribute to chest pain?
optionb: By straining the intercostal muscles and rib cage
hint: Slouching pulls on the chest framework.
detailedsolution: Poor positioning causes unnatural tension in the chest area.

questiontext: When walking, you should keep your head ______?
hint: Forward-looking is part of good posture.
detailedsolution: Looking forward helps maintain a straight neck and spine while walking.

questiontext: Proper sitting posture during eating prevents ______?
hint: It helps the food path stay clear.
detailedsolution: Correct alignment aids the swallowing and digestive process.

questiontext: Which mineral salt makes the enamel of teeth hard?
hint: Same as bones.
detailedsolution: Calcium is the primary mineral in the hard outer layer of teeth.

questiontext: Does regular exercise improve your posture?
optionc: Only if you are a baby
hint: Strong muscles hold the body better.
detailedsolution: Exercise strengthens the muscles that support good posture.

questiontext: Which of these is NOT a danger of bad posture?
hint: Bad posture actually weakens muscles.
detailedsolution: Stronger muscles are a result of good posture and exercise, not bad posture.

questiontext: What is the effect of bad posture on the spinal cord?
optionb: It can put unnatural pressure on it
hint: The spine protects the cord; if the spine bends, the cord suffers.
detailedsolution: Unnatural curves in the spine can compress the nerves of the spinal cord.

questiontext: To maintain healthy teeth, one should avoid too much ______?
hint: Sugar causes decay.
detailedsolution: Sugary foods react with bacteria to cause tooth decay.

questiontext: When standing, your shoulders should be ______?
hint: Natural position.
detailedsolution: Good standing posture involves keeping shoulders level and relaxed.

questiontext: How does having enough rest and sleep help the skeletal system?
optionb: It allows muscles to recover and bones to grow
hint: Growth happens mostly during rest.
detailedsolution: Rest is essential for the repair and development of muscles and bones.

questiontext: Which class of food is most necessary for bone and tooth growth?
hint: Body-building and strengthening.
detailedsolution: Proteins build tissue, while minerals like calcium strengthen bones and teeth.

questiontext: Bad posture while reading can cause ______?
hint: Think of bending your neck to see.
detailedsolution: Slouching while reading strains the muscles of the neck and eyes.

questiontext: Why should P.7 pupils carry their school bags on both shoulders?
optionb: To balance the weight and prevent spinal curving
hint: Uneven weight is bad for the spine.
detailedsolution: Carrying weight on one side causes the spine to bend unnaturally.

questiontext: Where do you sit during a lesson to have good posture?
optiona: On the edge of the chair
optionb: With your back against the chair's support
hint: Use the chair's design.
detailedsolution: The backrest of a chair is there to help you stay upright.

questiontext: How many times should you floss your teeth daily?
hint: Consistency is key.
detailedsolution: The notes recommend regular daily flossing (usually twice with brushing).

questiontext: What is the consequence of 'deformed bones' in adulthood?
optionb: They are permanent and cause lifelong pain
hint: Bones are harder and less changeable in adults.
detailedsolution: Postural deformities from childhood often become permanent in adults.

questiontext: A balanced diet for bones must include which vitamin?
hint: The "sunshine" vitamin.
detailedsolution: Vitamin D is required for the body to absorb calcium for bones and teeth.

questiontext: Walking with 'head up' is an example of?
hint: Looking forward.
detailedsolution: Keeping the head aligned with the spine is a sign of good posture.

questiontext: Which skeletal disorder is most linked to long-term bad posture?
hint: It's a curve of the upper spine.
detailedsolution: Kyphosis is often caused by chronic slouching.

questiontext: What is the best way to clean your mouth after eating?
hint: Remove the food leftovers.
detailedsolution: Rinsing or brushing after meals prevents bacteria from damaging teeth.

questiontext: Good posture prevents one from growing ______ bones?
hint: The opposite of straight.
detailedsolution: Children should maintain good posture to ensure bones grow straight.

questiontext: Bad posture affects which system responsible for taking in oxygen?
hint: Think about the lungs.
detailedsolution: A hunched chest limits the space for the respiratory system to work.

questiontext: If you feel a 'backache' after studying, you probably have ______?
hint: Slouching causes pain.
detailedsolution: Pain in the back is a common result of improper sitting positions.

questiontext: Sitting 'upright' means your spine is ______?
hint: The natural vertical line.
detailedsolution: An upright spine is the healthiest position for sitting.

questiontext: Which mineral deficiency can make both bones and teeth brittle?
hint: The building blocks of hard tissue.
detailedsolution: Both calcium and phosphorus are required to keep these structures strong.

questiontext: How does a person with good posture usually feel?
hint: Body systems work better.
detailedsolution: Efficiency of organs leads to feeling more energetic.

questiontext: Proper posture ensures that ______ and bones grow well?
hint: Muscles work with bones.
detailedsolution: The muscular and skeletal systems both benefit from correct body alignment.

questiontext: Why is it harder to correct posture in adults than in children?
hint: Think of dry clay vs wet clay.
detailedsolution: Children's bones are more flexible and still growing, making correction easier.

questiontext: Good posture is a ______ habit that everyone should learn?
hint: It benefits your whole life.
detailedsolution: Maintaining good posture is a fundamental healthy habit for physical well-being.

questiontext: What is a fracture?
hint: Think about what happens when a bone snaps.
detailedsolution: A fracture is defined as a cracked or broken bone in the body.

questiontext: Which type of fracture involves a bone breaking but remaining inside the flesh?
hint: It is also called a 'closed' fracture.
detailedsolution: A simple fracture occurs when the bone breaks but the skin is not pierced.

questiontext: What defines a compound fracture?
optiona: The bone is broken into many pieces
optionb: The bone breaks and comes out through the skin
hint: It is an 'open' fracture.
detailedsolution: A compound fracture is when the broken bone tears the flesh and is visible outside.

questiontext: Which fracture is most common in young children because their bones are soft?
hint: Think of a young, flexible tree branch.
detailedsolution: A greenstick fracture is when the bone bends and cracks but does not break completely.

questiontext: What happens in a comminuted fracture?
optiona: The bone breaks into several parts
optiond: It only happens in the skull
hint: Think of a bone being shattered.
detailedsolution: A comminuted fracture is a type of injury where the bone is broken into several pieces.

questiontext: What is a sprain?
hint: This injury happens at the joint.
detailedsolution: A sprain is a torn or overstretched ligament.

questiontext: What is a strain?
optiond: A disease caused by a virus
hint: Think of 'straining' your muscles.
detailedsolution: A strain is an injury specifically to a muscle or its tendon.

questiontext: What occurs during a dislocation?
optionb: Bones forming a joint are displaced from their normal position
hint: The bones 'pop out' of the joint.
detailedsolution: Dislocation is the displacement of bones at a joint.

questiontext: What is the first thing you should do when giving first aid for a fracture?
optionb: Remove any object that caused the fracture and stop bleeding
hint: Safety and stopping blood loss come first.
detailedsolution: Initial first aid involves removing hazards and controlling any bleeding.

questiontext: What is the purpose of a splint in first aid?
optionb: To keep the broken bones in position and prevent movement
optionc: To make the bone grow faster
optiond: To stop the person from crying
hint: It acts as a temporary support.
detailedsolution: A splint is used to immobilize a fractured limb to prevent further injury.

questiontext: What should be used to support a broken arm?
optionb: An arm sling tied around the neck
hint: It hangs from the neck.
detailedsolution: An arm sling is used to support and rest a broken arm.

questiontext: What does the acronym RICE stand for in treating dislocations or sprains?
hint: Check the Tekule notes page 23.
detailedsolution: RICE stands for Raise the injury, apply Ice, Compress with a bandage, and Elevate.

questiontext: Which of these is a sign of a fracture?
hint: Injured bones hurt a lot.
detailedsolution: Fractures are characterized by intense pain and inability to move the part.

questiontext: If a limb appears 'crooked' or 'shortened' after a fall, it is likely a?
hint: The shape of the bone has changed.
detailedsolution: Deformity or shortening of a limb is a common sign of a bone break.

questiontext: Why should you avoid tampering with a dislocated joint?
optiona: It might make the person laugh
optionb: It can cause further damage to nerves and blood vessels
hint: Only a doctor should put a bone back.
detailedsolution: Tampering can worsen the injury; professional medical help is required.

questiontext: What is used to keep a fractured bone from moving further?
hint: It is a stiff support.
detailedsolution: A splint prevents the ends of broken bones from moving and causing more pain.

questiontext: Knock-knees and bow legs are examples of?
hint: They are abnormal bone shapes.
detailedsolution: These are disorders where the legs grow in curved or bent shapes.

questiontext: What is the main difference between a sprain and a strain?
optiona: Sprain is bone, Strain is skin
optionb: Sprain is ligament, Strain is muscle
optionc: Sprain is muscle, Strain is ligament
hint: Think 'Ligament = Sprain'.
detailedsolution: A sprain affects the ligaments at a joint; a strain affects the muscles/tendons.

questiontext: Sudden swelling and bruising at a joint after a twist usually indicates?
hint: Typical after 'rolling' an ankle.
detailedsolution: Swelling and bruising are classic signs of a sprained ligament.

questiontext: In case of a compound fracture, why are antiseptics used?
optionb: To prevent infection of the open wound
hint: Open wounds attract germs.
detailedsolution: Antiseptics kill germs and prevent infections in open fractures.

questiontext: What is a club foot?
optionb: An abnormal foot shape present at birth
hint: It is a skeletal disorder.
detailedsolution: Club foot is a disorder where the foot is twisted out of its normal shape.

questiontext: Which tool is used to carry a person with a broken leg to the hospital?
hint: It keeps the body flat and still.
detailedsolution: A stretcher is the safest way to transport a victim with a major fracture.

questiontext: 'Elevation' in the RICE method helps to?
hint: Lifting the limb above the heart.
detailedsolution: Elevating the injured part helps drain fluid and reduce swelling.

questiontext: A person with 'bow legs' has legs that curve?
hint: Think of the shape of a bow.
detailedsolution: Bow legs curve outward at the knees.

questiontext: Which first aid equipment is tied around the neck to support a broken wrist?
hint: It cradles the arm.
detailedsolution: An arm sling provides rest and support for wrist or arm injuries.

questiontext: What is a sign of a compound fracture that you won't see in a simple fracture?
optionb: Bleeding and bone pushing through skin
hint: One is 'open', one is 'closed'.
detailedsolution: Only compound fractures involve an open wound with visible bone.

questiontext: To 'compress' an injury means to?
hint: Applying pressure.
detailedsolution: Compression involves using a bandage to provide support and limit swelling.

questiontext: Why is ice applied to a sprained ankle?
hint: Cold narrows blood vessels.
detailedsolution: Ice packs help manage the immediate pain and inflammation of a sprain.

questiontext: Which of these is a skeletal disorder?
hint: It affects the bones.
detailedsolution: Knock-knees is a condition where the knees touch but ankles stay apart.

questiontext: What is the first aid for a strain (muscle injury)?
optionb: Apply a cold compress or ice pack
hint: Cold helps soothe muscles.
detailedsolution: Applying ice helps treat the pain of a torn or overstretched muscle.

questiontext: How can you identify a dislocation without an X-ray?
optiona: The joint looks visibly out of place
hint: It looks 'deformed'.
detailedsolution: A dislocated joint will appear visibly deformed compared to the normal side.

questiontext: If someone breaks a leg, you should ______?
optiona: Tell them to walk it off
optionb: Keep them still and call for help
optiond: Cover the whole body in salt
hint: Immobilization is key.
detailedsolution: Movement can make a fracture worse; keep the patient still.

questiontext: A 'grating' or 'grinding' sound when moving a limb after a fall suggests?
hint: Bone ends rubbing together.
detailedsolution: The sensation of bone ends rubbing is a specific sign of a fracture.

questiontext: Why is it important to 'give comfort and assurance' to an injured person?
optionb: To help reduce shock and keep them calm
optionc: To make the bone heal instantly
hint: Mental state affects physical recovery.
detailedsolution: Calming the victim is a vital part of first aid to prevent shock.

questiontext: Which professional should treat all skeletal injuries?
hint: They are trained in medicine.
detailedsolution: Always seek medical attention from a doctor for bone or joint injuries.

questiontext: What is the common name for the displacement of bones at a joint?
hint: The bones are 'dis-located'.
detailedsolution: Dislocation occurs when bones are forced out of their natural socket.

questiontext: In the RICE method, what does 'C' stand for?
hint: Using a bandage for pressure.
detailedsolution: Compression helps limit internal bleeding and swelling.

questiontext: Can you see the bone in a simple fracture?
hint: The skin remains intact.
detailedsolution: In a simple (closed) fracture, the bone stays inside the body.

questiontext: A 'torn ligament' is another name for a?
hint: Affects the joint connector.
detailedsolution: A sprain is specifically an injury to the ligaments.

questiontext: What should you do before applying a splint to a compound fracture?
optionb: Stop the bleeding and cover the wound
hint: Wound care first.
detailedsolution: Open wounds must be managed before immobilizing the limb.

questiontext: A fracture where the bone is shattered into many bits is ______?
hint: Think of many fragments.
detailedsolution: Comminuted fractures involve the bone breaking into several parts.

questiontext: What is the use of crutches?
optiona: To help an injured person move
hint: Support for walking.
detailedsolution: Crutches allow a victim with a leg injury to move without putting weight on it.

questiontext: Why are greenstick fractures common in children?
optionb: Because children's bones are more cartilaginous and soft
hint: Think about bone hardening (ossification).
detailedsolution: Children's bones haven't fully hardened, so they bend rather than snapping clean.

questiontext: What is the best way to carry a patient with a spinal injury?
hint: The spine must be kept perfectly straight.
detailedsolution: Spinal injuries require a firm, flat surface for transport to prevent paralysis.

questiontext: Muscle cramps are considered a ______ system disorder?
hint: It affects the flesh.
detailedsolution: Cramps are painful, involuntary contractions of the muscular system.

questiontext: What is a likely result of not immobilizing a fracture?
optionb: Internal bleeding and further tissue damage
hint: Moving broken bones is dangerous.
detailedsolution: Sharp bone ends can cut muscles and blood vessels if not kept still.

questiontext: What does 'Elevate' mean in the treatment of a sprain?
hint: Keeping it high.
detailedsolution: Elevation means raising the injured part above heart level.

questiontext: Dislocation is common in which parts of the body?
hint: Movable joints are most at risk.
detailedsolution: Movable joints like shoulders and fingers are prone to being forced out of place.

questiontext: In case of a sprained wrist, what is applied for support?
hint: Think of immobilizing the arm.
detailedsolution: The arm sling keeps the wrist supported and still.

questiontext: What happens when a bone is displaced?
hint: The bone has moved from its home.
detailedsolution: Displacement of bones at a joint defines a dislocation.

questiontext: Which of these symptoms is shared by fractures, sprains, and dislocations?
optionb: Difficulty in moving the affected part
hint: Injury prevents normal use.
detailedsolution: Pain and restricted movement are common to all major musculoskeletal injuries.

questiontext: What does 'Rest' in RICE help achieve?
optionb: Prevents further injury to the damaged tissue
hint: Stopping activity allows healing to start.
detailedsolution: Resting prevents worsening the injury by stopping movement of the affected area.

questiontext: Which type of fracture involves a shattered bone?
hint: Think of 'crushed'.
detailedsolution: A comminuted fracture is when the bone is broken into several fragments.

questiontext: Tying an arm sling around the neck provides ______ for a broken arm?
hint: It holds the arm for you.
detailedsolution: The sling supports the weight of the arm and prevents it from moving.

questiontext: What should you NOT do when someone has a compound fracture?
optionb: Try to push the bone back inside
optionc: Cover the wound with a clean cloth
hint: This is very dangerous.
detailedsolution: Never attempt to reset a bone yourself; wait for a medical professional.

questiontext: Bruising is a sign of?
hint: Discoloration under the skin.
detailedsolution: Bruising happens when blood vessels under the skin break due to injury.

questiontext: Which type of fracture is also called an 'Open Fracture'?
hint: The skin is open.
detailedsolution: A compound fracture is 'open' because the skin is pierced.

questiontext: How does ice help a dislocated joint?
optionb: It numbs the pain and limits swelling
optiond: It makes it easier to pull
hint: Cold is a natural painkiller.
detailedsolution: Cold reduces blood flow to the area, minimizing swelling and pain.

questiontext: Which of these is the correct first aid tool for a leg break?
hint: It keeps the leg straight.
detailedsolution: A splint is necessary to keep a broken leg stable during transport.

questiontext: Severe pain at a joint with visible deformity is a sign of?
hint: The bone looks 'wrong'.
detailedsolution: Deformity at a joint is the primary sign that bones have moved out of place.

questiontext: Which of the following is a disease caused by a virus?
hint: It can cause paralysis of the limbs.
detailedsolution: Polio (Poliomyelitis) is caused by the polio virus.

questiontext: What is the cause of Rickets in children?
optionb: Lack of Vitamin D and Calcium
hint: Think about a nutrient deficiency.
detailedsolution: Rickets is a deficiency disease caused by a lack of Vitamin D, Calcium, or Phosphorus.

questiontext: Which disease is caused by bacteria and leads to loss of feeling in the skin?
hint: It also causes shortening of fingers and toes.
detailedsolution: Leprosy is a bacterial disease that affects the skin, nerves, and bones.

questiontext: How is Polio spread from one person to another?
hint: Think about hygiene and germs.
detailedsolution: Polio germs are spread through drinking water contaminated with infected faeces.

questiontext: A child with 'bowlegs' is likely suffering from which disease?
hint: This disease makes bones weak and soft.
detailedsolution: Rickets causes leg bones to grow in curved shapes like bowlegs or knock-knees.

questiontext: Which vaccine is given to children to prevent Polio?
hint: The name of the vaccine matches the disease.
detailedsolution: Children are immunized against Polio using the polio vaccine (usually drops in the mouth).

questiontext: Tuberculosis of the spine is caused by a bacterium called?
hint: It shares a name with the lung disease.
detailedsolution: TB of the bones/spine is caused by the bacterium Mycobacterium tuberculosis.

questiontext: What is a common sign of Tuberculosis of the spine?
optionb: A lump growing on the spine
hint: It affects the backbone's shape.
detailedsolution: A physical lump on the back and persistent backache are signs of spinal TB.

questiontext: Which disease is caused by bacteria found in the soil entering through cuts?
hint: It makes muscles very stiff.
detailedsolution: Tetanus bacteria (Clostridium tetani) live in the soil and enter via open wounds.

questiontext: Which vaccine prevents Tetanus?
hint: It is a triple vaccine.
detailedsolution: The DPT (Diphtheria, Pertussis, Tetanus) vaccine protects against Tetanus.

questiontext: Which disease causes a person to lose feeling in parts of their skin?
hint: It is an infectious bacterial disease.
detailedsolution: A key symptom of Leprosy is the loss of sensation in the skin.

questiontext: How can TB of the bones be spread to humans from animals?
optionb: By drinking unboiled milk from an infected cow
hint: Milk must be boiled or pasteurized.
detailedsolution: TB bacteria can be found in the milk of infected cows.

questiontext: What is the adult version of Rickets called?
hint: It starts with 'Osteo'.
detailedsolution: Osteomalacia is the lack of Vitamin D and calcium in adults, leading to fragile bones.

questiontext: Which disease is characterized by 'stiff muscles all over the body'?
hint: It is caused by germs in wounds.
detailedsolution: Tetanus causes painful muscle spasms and stiffness (lockjaw).

questiontext: To prevent Polio, we should always drink ______ water?
hint: Boiling kills the virus.
detailedsolution: Boiling water is a primary way to prevent the spread of water-borne polio germs.

questiontext: Which mineral deficiency leads to poor teeth formation and weak bones?
hint: Found in milk and fish.
detailedsolution: Calcium and phosphorus are essential for building strong bone tissue.

questiontext: The BCG vaccine is given to prevent which disease?
hint: It is given on the right upper arm at birth.
detailedsolution: BCG (Bacillus Calmette-Guérin) is the vaccine for Tuberculosis.

questiontext: What is the scientific name for the bacteria that cause Tetanus?
hint: Look at the name carefully.
detailedsolution: Tetanus is caused by Clostridium tetani.

questiontext: Which disease causes the shortening of fingers and toes if not treated?
hint: It affects the extremities of the body.
detailedsolution: Leprosy can cause physical deformities like shortened fingers.

questiontext: Why is Polio called a disease of the limbs?
optiona: Because it only affects the arms
optionb: Because it weakens or paralyses leg muscles
optionc: Because it makes limbs grow longer
hint: Paralysis leads to lameness.
detailedsolution: Polio virus attacks nerves, leading to muscle weakness or paralysis in the limbs.

questiontext: Which disease is an inherited condition where voluntary muscles progressively weaken?
hint: Check the P.7 Tekule notes page 18.
detailedsolution: Muscular dystrophy is a group of inherited diseases that weaken muscles.

questiontext: Inflammation, pain, and stiffness of the joints is called?
hint: Common in older people or after injury.
detailedsolution: Arthritis is the disease characterized by painful and stiff joints.

questiontext: Which disease is caused by thinning of bones due to hormone imbalance, especially in women?
hint: Bones become very brittle.
detailedsolution: Osteoporosis involves a loss of bone mineral density and tissue.

questiontext: A person with Rickets should eat more foods rich in?
hint: These nutrients fix the deficiency.
detailedsolution: Vitamin D from sun/food and calcium from milk are the cure/prevention.

questiontext: What is a symptom of Leprosy affecting the skin?
hint: The nerves in the skin are damaged.
detailedsolution: A major sign of leprosy is the loss of sensation in skin patches.

questiontext: What is 'Osteoarthritis' primarily caused by?
optionc: Daily wear and tear of the joint
hint: It happens over time as we age.
detailedsolution: Osteoarthritis is a degenerative joint disease from long-term use and injury.

questiontext: Bone cancer is characterized by what?
hint: This is how all cancers work.
detailedsolution: Cancer of the bones involves the uncontrolled multiplication of bone cells.
